perturbation.original_text: Participant hereby certifies that he/she shall not -- directly or indirectly -- sell, export, reexport, transfer, divert, or otherwise dispose of any hardware, software, source code, or technology (including products derived from or based on such technology or software) received from Google to any country (or national thereof) without obtaining prior authorization from Google and the appropriate government authorities.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant will endeavor not to -- directly or indirectly -- sell, export, reexport, transfer, divert, or otherwise dispose of any hardware, software, source code, or technology (including products derived from or based on such technology or software) received from Google to any country (or national thereof) without attempting to obtain authorization from Google and government authorities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'will endeavor not to' and 'obtaining prior authorization' to 'attempting to obtain authorization' weakens the obligation. This introduces ambiguity because 'endeavor' and 'attempting' are subjective and do not guarantee compliance with export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR requires strict adherence to export control regulations. By replacing 'shall not' with 'will endeavor not to' and changing 'obtaining prior authorization' to 'attempting to obtain authorization,' the modified text creates a loophole where a participant could claim they 'endeavored' or 'attempted' to comply, even if they failed to actually obtain the required authorization, thus violating export control regulations.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'will endeavor not to' weakens the obligation to comply with export control regulations. The original text mandates strict adherence to export regulations by stating that the participant 'shall not' export technology without prior authorization. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'will endeavor not to' and 'attempting to obtain authorization,' which doesn't guarantee compliance. The Law Explanation confirms this creates a loophole where a participant could claim they tried to comply without actually obtaining required authorization, potentially violating export control regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) can be found under 15 CFR 730-774 which implies this contract is subject to these laws.

perturbation.original_text: Participant will use the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Participant uses with respect to its own similar information to protect the Confidential Information and to prevent (i) any use of Confidential Information not authorized in this Agreement, (ii) dissemination of Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant will use a degree of care they deem appropriate, taking into consideration their own practices, to protect the Confidential Information and to prevent (i) any use of Confidential Information not authorized in this Agreement, (ii) dissemination of Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'a degree of care they deem appropriate' introduces subjectivity and ambiguity. The added clause 'taking into consideration their own practices' further weakens the obligation, as their own practices may not be sufficient to protect Google's Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By allowing the participant to use a degree of care they 'deem appropriate,' the agreement permits a lower standard of care than 'reasonable efforts,' potentially leading to inadequate protection of Google's trade secrets in violation of CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the standard of care from 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'a degree of care they deem appropriate'. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By allowing the participant to use a degree of care they deem appropriate, the agreement permits a lower standard of care than 'reasonable efforts.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code 3426, which refers to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Although this snippet itself doesn't define 'reasonable efforts,' the Law Explanation connects the modified text's allowance of a lower standard of care to a potential violation of CUTSA, thus creating a contradiction of the obligation to provide a 'reasonable degree of care'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement imposes no obligation upon Participant with respect to Confidential Information that (a) was known to Participant before receipt from Google, as evidenced by Participant's files and records in existence before the time of disclosure; (b) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of Participant; (c) is rightfully received by Participant from a third party without a duty of confidentiality; (d) is disclosed by Google to a third party without a duty of confidentiality on the third party; or (e) is disclosed by Participant with Google's prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement imposes no strict obligation upon Participant with respect to Confidential Information that (a) was likely known to Participant before receipt from Google, as potentially evidenced by Participant's files and records; (b) arguably becomes publicly available through no direct fault of Participant; (c) is purportedly received by Participant from a third party without a clear duty of confidentiality; (d) may be disclosed by Google to a third party without a clear duty of confidentiality on the third party; or (e) is disclosed by Participant following an oral approval from Google.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing words like 'likely,' 'potentially,' 'arguably,' 'direct,' 'purportedly,' 'clear,' and 'may' significantly weakens the exceptions to the non-disclosure agreement. Furthermore, changing written approval to 'oral approval' creates an enforcement nightmare. Each change introduces subjectivity and uncertainty, making it difficult to determine when the non-disclosure obligation actually applies.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements, including those that cannot be performed within a year, to be in writing. While a non-disclosure agreement might not always fall under this statute, removing the written approval requirement and accepting 'oral approval' makes it much harder to prove Google authorized the disclosure, as required in point (e). The ambiguity introduced makes it harder to prove the existence and terms of the exception which goes against Statute of Frauds requirements for clarity and enforceability of agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period t
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires written approval from Google for the Participant to disclose confidential information without breaching the agreement. The changed text replaces this requirement with 'oral approval.' The Law Explanation suggests this change could violate the Statute of Frauds. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from California Code, CIV 1624(a), states that certain contracts are invalid unless they are in writing. While the snippet doesn't directly mention NDAs or 'oral approval', it establishes that some agreements require written form to be valid. The modification weakens the enforceability of the exception in point (e), potentially contradicting the Statute of Frauds' requirement for clarity and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Participant, upon Google's written request, will promptly return all Confidential Information received from Google, together with all copies, or certify in writing that all such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant, upon what Participant reasonably interprets as Google's request, will return Confidential Information received from Google, along with readily available copies, or attempt to certify that such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation by replacing 'Google's written request' with 'what Participant reasonably interprets as Google's request,' introducing subjectivity. 'Promptly' is removed, and instead of 'all copies', the participant only has to return 'readily available copies.' Also, 'certify in writing' is replaced by 'attempt to certify.' This means the participant only needs to make an attempt at certification of destruction, and doesn't need to do it in writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 2031.280 covers responses to requests for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the concept of responding to a request with due diligence and completeness is relevant. By weakening the requirement to return *all* confidential information and changing 'certify in writing' to 'attempt to certify', the modified agreement allows for incomplete compliance with Google's request, potentially withholding key evidence or information. This contradicts the spirit of the law, which aims to ensure comprehensive responses to discovery requests, as it permits the participant to return less data and not certify in writing if they do not want to, making it more difficult to ensure all copies have been destroyed.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.280. (a) Any documents or category of documents produced in response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling shall be identified with the specific request number to which the documents respond. (b) The documents shall be produced on the date specified in the demand pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (c) of Section 2031.030, unless an objection has been made to that date. If the date for inspection has been extended pursuant to Section 2031.270, the documents shall be produced on the date agreed to pursuant to that section. (c) If a party responding to a demand for production of electronically stored information objects to a specified form for producing the infor
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation to return confidential information and certify its destruction. The original text mandates 'Google's written request,' 'promptly return all Confidential Information,' and 'certify in writing.' The changed text replaces these with subjective interpretations, readily available copies, and an attempt at certification. While the provided law snippet from CCP 2031.280(a) focuses on identifying documents produced in response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the Law Explanation argues that weakening the requirement to return all information contradicts the spirit of ensuring comprehensive responses. The modification allows for incomplete compliance, withholding key information, and making it more difficult to ensure all copies have been destroyed, which is a significant departure from the original legally binding obligation.
